speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. Now, we're still in Warsaw, and your friends now are having trouble on the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're also still working. And we're in 1940. What's happening on the streets in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- 1940, Warsaw-- Warsaw City was bombed. Bricks was laid down on the street. And there was very few building-- building standing, mostly-- mostly broken. Then, there was a beautiful day at the time, I believe in May, June, or July. I was arrested. There was plenty of people in Warsaw, you know, that was walking, and the-- then the German army, SS, was walking down the street too, and somehow, so quick they come in like a convoy, SS. Was [INAUDIBLE], I don't know what kind of [? army, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Because in my paper, they say I was arrested by [INAUDIBLE]. And everybody, don't move, stay in your place. And everybody arrested, children, older people, Jewish people, Jewish Polish people, Polish people, makes no difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no protest going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. So that's-- that's why the first convoy, from Warsaw, 1940, they brought us straight to-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you were on the street. It was daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daytime, beautiful day. Before-- before noon time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before noon time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no awareness that anything was going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: One lady told me, she told me about [INAUDIBLE], be careful, watch-- watch because [INAUDIBLE] arrest. I said-- I said nothing happened to me. I just ignore them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were no massive arrests before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was first massive arrest that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: On that day, in June of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: June, I believe, 12th, June or July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they just took you up, children, old people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, everybody, even people who even hardly could walk, older people, like-- like Polish Jewish people. For them, there was no mercy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Threw you on this truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- no, we marched it. We marched it. And by the-- by night to the-- I think it was [INAUDIBLE]. It was Polish cavalry, first Polish cavalry was before the war. They-- they hold us on the saw dust, because that was for the horses. For three days, we're sitting, on a middle, those-- those are machine guns, and nobody could-- could move no place. And after three days--

speaker: interviewer
text: No food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe throw it just like, like you throw it to the zoo, to the lions or tiger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're staying at these stables of the cavalry.

speaker: interviewee
text: For three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people, a guess?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe maybe 4,000, 5,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after three days, the-- the-- the-- I believe, if I remember, they put us on the trucks to the-- to the Praga, that's Praga that's divided, like-- like Manhattan and Brooklyn, because divided by the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: District.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they travel-- travel us to the station. And I remember truck, there was truck, and an SS motorcycle with a machine gun, truck, SS with a machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put you on the train. What kind of train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freight train? Men, women--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children, old, young--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody. It was-- like a sardine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Closed in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No place to even-- even sit because it was so tight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three, or three and a half, or four days. There was many escape, many times the train stopped. Many-- many machine guns shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody escape do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, some escape. Some were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew anybody on the train? Were you with anyone you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was so many people. Because I was-- I was only one, [INAUDIBLE] [? too. ?] So nobody know me, I don't know nobody. I know there was Polish Jewish, Polish, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: People talk to each other on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a calm in the train, or was-- were people very upset and agitated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sadness. Some people knew already the [INAUDIBLE] Auschwitz. To me, I had no knowledge, nothing. I never even knew that Auschwitz existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had an idea what direction they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some people guessing, because I hear when-- when there was talking, [? the older ?] people, so we're going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody die on the train? It was very hot, I assume.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many people died, fainted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you bearing up now? How were you feeling on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very tough, very strong. So it doesn't bother me. Even I could stand 24 hours on own feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have any food in your pocket, anything to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe some chocolate. I'm not sure, because usually when I was there, walking the street, there was a chunk of sugar, Polish sugar that was given for the horses, I carried [INAUDIBLE] chocolate, very-- a very famous chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had something to hold you. Did they feed you at all, put something into the doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe they give bread [BACKROUND NOISE] and maybe water. I'm not so sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you're three, four days on the plane-- on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are arrive--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your first impression as you're walking off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared to death. Like a-- when I watch it now on a movie, it was much worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it look-- now we're in 1940-- we're very early-- did it look in 1940 the same way as it looked in 1945? Was it smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in 1940-- it was very few people. And a couple thousand people in Auschwitz. And it was very strictly, the SS was monsters. We would be nothing. We would be nothing [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get into Auschwitz, you're still at the train, now, you look outside the train as you're walking down from the train, who do you see in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly SS. [INAUDIBLE] SS were there. Dogs with a man, all kind of different gestapo, SS, and there was about three four branches of their army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they placing the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were walking on their arms, all-- all around there by the-- by the freighter cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any prisoners when you walked off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we been already in the-- in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all in the train. And when you got there, you only saw German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two or three, small spaces, because the freighter car was not tight [INAUDIBLE] you could watch through the-- through the spaces.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you're off the train. Did they take everybody to the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go? The first place you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Directly to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz, did you go to a bath? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Went to-- very hot in Auschwitz. People fainted. There was not enough water to drink. And everybody gets slapped, kick, or even killed-- pushed with a rifle to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they shoot anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard some shots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were they moving you toward? What direction?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz, there was a train stop on the ramp, very high, almost one flight, the the [? freighter ?] was open on a [INAUDIBLE]. And the German, they say [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That's German. I'll never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to come down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- we had to jump it, almost one story because the ramp was so high, and we jump it. Some people when they jump it, got broken leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they still had to move on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people could not pick up, and several German come and kick-- kick them, and-- and hit them with a rifle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they move to? Where were you going? All this-- all these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody would line up, I believe five or 10, line up, and they marched directly into Auschwitz. [? Where it say ?] Arbeit Macht Frei.

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually went through the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they bring you?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a-- everybody stand on a-- on a-- in the middle of Auschwitz, on a place. Then the [GERMAN] SS man, [GERMAN] that's like [INAUDIBLE]. [GERMAN] his name was Palitzsch, [INAUDIBLE]. He was an SS, much higher in rank. We were scared of him to death. When he come on a motorcycle from Auschwitz to Birkenau, and I was witness. Four or five SS men was standing and talking between themselves. When the-- the one of the SS say, [INAUDIBLE] Palitzsch, the SS split.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the first person you meet in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in this piazza.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then [INAUDIBLE] take a big log book and call each-- each name, by name. From-- they kept everything, [INAUDIBLE], name, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took this at-- at-- in Warsaw, already, your names?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, I believe, name, when sitting in the stable place. And then [INAUDIBLE] wrote every name to-- to they have to come to him and say I am [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] means professional. What's your profession? [GERMAN] They said, good. [GERMAN], doctor, kick, slap. [SPEAKING GERMAN] a student. Very good. [GERMAN], name, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] Very good. [GERMAN] Lawyer, attorney, slap, kick. Mostly intelligent people being punished. Right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they separate people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Separated people.

speaker: interviewer
text: According to their profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN], a student.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: With young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special barracks for the young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven, number seven barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barrack seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like? Describe it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Set ?] Barracks, black, [? sadness. ?] Long hall. And it--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- where did you sleep in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no bunk. You sleep on the concrete in the-- in the straw, mattress, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the barrack made of wood or stone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was bricks, bricks. Auschwitz was bricks. At that time, there was a single building. Then after, when the-- been work-- people would be working 24 hours, day and night, they shift. They make it two, three story, higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you already had a built barrack when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there-- there-- because in Auschwitz, there was Polish-- Polish army was in Auschwitz, those single-- single building, one story. When we get there, there was not enough room. There were already architect from Berlin come, they have to take off the roofs, make them two, three story higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came there, it was still one story?

speaker: interviewee
text: One story, like it was, you know, there was-- from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you slept on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your barrack?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1,100 in your own, in one barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 1,200.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sleep like a sardine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I guess. In the summer, there was-- it was very hot. Impossible to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept your clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there-- there-- our clothes take away from us. At the beginning, we-- we be in our clothes, but when we went to take shower, I don't know when, next day maybe, they take away our clothes. We get the prisoner clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like, the prisoner clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like you saw [INAUDIBLE], blue and white, on some like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stripes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they always the same? Did they always look the same, the uniforms, or did they change?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the winter time, we got different clothes. They were more heavy. In the summertime, you had the more light, blue, white, a little bit lighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sent to this barrack. Did they feed you in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a cook-- cooks, there was-- there was kitchen already made. They made us some kind of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were already prisoners there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was already prisoner already. There was first prison, there was from Krakow. In the-- [INAUDIBLE]. They coming from [INAUDIBLE]. There a-- a couple hundred people, Polish people already from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you not-- you were the first big transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were big-- big-- were the first transport from the Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a transport from Krakow before you?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Krakow and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the number, there was one number, Polish man was number one. They had 2,000. That was-- he was before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've seen a man with the number one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Number one, number three, number four. Because there was-- start from number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had a number on your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the uniform, on a small cloth. Whatever the [INAUDIBLE], and I believe Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you designated a type of prisoner? Were you a political prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I was a political prisoner, because-- which-- I belonged to no [INAUDIBLE] organization because I was too young. But as-- as-- to German, I was as political, because I was very active when I been in the civil life, the German put me in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- but after they arrested you, they knew that you were a Polish student.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew I was Polish student. Then they knew everything about me. I was-- I don't belong to any organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they still wouldn't let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew your family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no time for you at all, your parents didn't know what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but my-- my parents was afraid to write to me at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't contact you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know what was happening with them either.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the population think about Auschwitz? I mean, why-- why did your parents not write you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew-- they were scared. Maybe-- they [INAUDIBLE]. That's why they were scared to contact me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, in your barrack with all the students, were there people from other places, or just Poland at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, there was Polish Jewish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same barrack, together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't separate the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- at the beginning, first, one, two days, there was disorganize.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what happened after the second day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe we went to-- we take bathtub, shower. And I believe there was kind of separation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Separation of whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: As-- as-- as far as I remember, separation of Polish, Polish Jewish and Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, they [INAUDIBLE] women, only Polish, Polish people. Polish Jewish, Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And children?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got me. I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just think in your memory what you saw there. Maybe they didn't take--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I know they arrested everybody, woman, not women, children, not children, but what happened with them, I don't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, from this moment in Auschwitz, you see men, and young men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Older people too. In your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they separated, started-- they separated only the Poles and the Jews, or did they separate the young and the old?

speaker: interviewee
text: They separated-- in the beginning, there was nothing-- everybody went all through to-- to the Auschwitz. Later, they separated, when they called each person by name. And they have to tell [INAUDIBLE] and the profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when they started separating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then started separating.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You still had a number on your--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, there was nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No number yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you given a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple days later, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you given the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the transport serial, like the transport was 4,000. I would say counted from one, to 2,254.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your number was 2,254.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you had a P.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe P, a red triangle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a red triangle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was on the left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on-- on the-- on the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewn on your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the sewing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Profession-- we have all kind of people in Auschwitz, believe me. Profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, after you got your number, what did they do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was-- there was not much to do. We [INAUDIBLE] pick up the grass, where they make bricks. On a hot, sunny day, and clean the [? alley, ?] you know, things like that. Just given-- do-- do something, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, whatever they asked you to do was not worth it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was two [? monsters, ?] I tell you. They-- like I said, lager-- lageralteste, maybe familiar to you? There was Bruno and Leo. Bruno was short, captain from a submarine, German submarine. Leo was about 300 or 400 pound, tall, tall man. [? Monstruous. ?] Also, I believe captain from submarine. And they hid-- hid behind the [? barrack ?] during the [INAUDIBLE] time, both of them, when they were in the concentration camp, Auschwitz, they let them go from prisoner to be [? masters ?] in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were prisoners during the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your-- the head of your-- your-- your barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those was two [? monsters, ?] Leo and Bruno.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they kill you, they do anything. Even-- even they kill German, their friend. They torture and [INAUDIBLE] unbelievable people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were staying there, you're in fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even I was struck a couple times by him. And then-- and then during the lunchtime, by the-- by the [INAUDIBLE]. Because he was so powerful. When he struck, with one [INAUDIBLE] people [INAUDIBLE] on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he strike you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- maybe-- maybe I annoyed him or something, that's why. And the people, the Polish people say, [INAUDIBLE], make sure-- make sure he hits you, [INAUDIBLE] fall down. Then I-- I realized, otherwise he'll hit me about six times, I was so strong. Because usually when he struck someone, the men-- men fly down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw killings in the barrack too. They-- they actually killed people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, killing, like you see the baseball, they play, from the handle from a shovel, I think, people been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They walked around with these bats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly the kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who were these-- not kapos--

speaker: interviewee
text: These were lageralteste, lageralteste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: One, like [INAUDIBLE]. Like I say, [LAUGHS] I call him [INAUDIBLE]. Lageralteste number one, Bruno. And lageralteste number two, Leo. There was three rules, only was [INAUDIBLE] to SS. Those civilian-- civilian prisoner, they-- they could do whatever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now. There was another person, who was a kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapo work on the commando, on the work. [? There was ?] kapo they calling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So soon, you started to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon, I started to work. [INAUDIBLE]. First I work in a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the commando. I-- I work in a commando, separate to the houses, because on periphery Auschwitz, I believe 40 to 60 kilometers square. I think people who live in beautiful houses have to leave the houses, and they move out or put them in the concentration camp. And the houses was beautiful, beautiful, beautiful. Most of the rich people before the war lived in-- in [INAUDIBLE] Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with these houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to break it into pieces and make it even ground so-- so it could be observed-- what you call it-- make better view.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah-ha. So you cleared the whole area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your assignment, your first assignment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was my-- I work for the commando. And there was, I don't know, a couple-- a couple thousand people been working at different houses, different [INAUDIBLE] place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did more people come every day, from trains? Did more people show--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, after that, everyday. I hear that people come, not only from Poland, France Jewish, from France, French people, from Belgium, Holland. Auschwitz was, I tell you, Auschwitz was very important. I-- there was-- like-- like in a plan made already. Auschwitz as excellent transportation from-- from entire Europe to Auschwitz. Even from Moscow. They made a special truck, because Moscow-- a Russian truck-- truck is much wider. So they made it from Moscow to-- to Auschwitz. Better connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: So already in 1940, you started having people from all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1940, Auschwitz was impossible to-- to be alive. Some people, intelligent, get crazy, get emotion, not stable, mostly intelligent people, like a professor, scientist, doctor, in two or three weeks, they get really crazy. So they don't care they-- what happened to him. So they went on electric-- electric [? fence, ?] get-- get-- get [INAUDIBLE] get shock or get shot at by tower, by the SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would the tower shoot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially on a holiday, the SS, [INAUDIBLE] to-- to shoot a haftling, prisoner. So the German Wehrmacht, the German SS, got vacation, two or three weeks, or more, for that reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could be shot at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anytime, anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all aware of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- even the people going so close, the electric fence was so powerful, if-- if I stand that-- that close to the-- to that [INAUDIBLE], the electric pull me to the fence. The [? electroactive ?] so powerful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you worked, how many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very difficult for me, long days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Long days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime, we-- on the lunchtime, we come to-- to Auschwitz. They feed us. Sometimes when we worked far away, they-- they bring them the soup to the place where we'd been working, like a [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] been working on their [INAUDIBLE]. Different-- different places. Auschwitz, I want you know, Auschwitz is-- was a very rich place. Auschwitz could support themselves. Auschwitz was very rich. There was race horses, race pigs, a mill, flour mill in Auschwitz. Fishing, it was-- it was really-- no, Auschwitz was absolutely independent, like New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't-- you didn't get any of that food. What kind of food did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, there was like corn flakes, some kind of spinach. In the beginning, it was different food, and later, it was very bad food.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning, it was much better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we will stop this tape.
